phases.role_line: Maps the recurring TBI mechanism lanes the rest of the engine builds on.
Shows which mechanisms are likely driving what happens next.
Tracks the recurring downstream damage states that matter over time.
Attaches targets, readouts, and intervention logic to the mechanism map.
Splits TBI into cohort and endotype patterns instead of one generic disease bucket.
Turns the structured engine into a ranked decision slate for the human operator.

major_findings: The lead mechanism in the live atlas is Mitochondrial Dysfunction.
The strongest supported bridge right now is BBB permeability increase -> peripheral immune infiltration.

The current lead insight is that Blood-Brain Barrier Failure biomarker panel is the best readout set to carry the next paper story, because this panel is already attached to a translational packet and can be tested against 4 endotype packet(s).
current_unknowns: The top current uncertainty is Clarify Chronic Repetitive Tau / Clearance Mixed.
Phase 4 is still bounded. It has 6 bounded packet(s), 1 lane(s) with compound support, and 1 lane(s) with trial support.
The biggest paper blocker right now is this: Barrier readouts can improve in tissue without reducing leakage or downstream inflammatory spillover.

phase_timeline.title: Phase 1 · Process Lanes
phase_timeline.summary: The engine reduced the TBI literature into six recurring process lanes so the project stopped treating every paper as a one-off mechanism note.
phase_timeline.detail: Today’s decisions only make sense because the lane map already tells us which biology is actually in play.

phase_timeline.title: Phase 2 · Causal Transitions
phase_timeline.summary: The engine converted lane-level mechanisms into explicit causal bridges, so the system can say what likely drives what next.
phase_timeline.detail: Current hinge and leverage decisions are built on these bridges.

phase_timeline.title: Phase 3 · Progression Objects
phase_timeline.summary: The engine grouped recurring downstream damage states into progression objects such as chronic network dysfunction and neurovascular uncoupling.
phase_timeline.detail: The current decision slate uses these objects to explain why a choice matters downstream.

phase_timeline.title: Phase 4 · Translational Logic
phase_timeline.summary: The engine attached translational packets to each lane so targets, readouts, and intervention logic sit on top of the mechanism map.
phase_timeline.detail: Biomarker panels and leverage-point choices come directly from these packets.

phase_timeline.title: Phase 5 · Cohort Stratification
phase_timeline.summary: The engine split TBI into cohort and endotype patterns so mild, repetitive, blast, severe, acute, and chronic states stop collapsing into one disease bucket.
phase_timeline.detail: The current ambiguity around tau versus clearance is an endotype problem, not just a mechanism problem.

phase_timeline.title: Phase 6 · Hypothesis Rankings
phase_timeline.summary: The engine ranked the strongest bridges, weakest hinges, biomarker panels, leverage points, and next tasks into a weekly decision slate.
phase_timeline.detail: The portal now uses this slate as the human decision queue.

snapshot.notes: Manual capture from the official Journal of Neurotrauma instructions plus the supplied April 2026 article-type-specific transparency statements for fluid biomarker papers and pathophysiological mechanistic studies. This journal explicitly considers original research, reviews, short communications, and letters to the editor; case reports are not accepted. It accepts format-neutral first submissions but still requires IRB/disclosure elements and a 500-word Transparency, Rigor, and Reproducibility Summary after the conclusion. Operational fit rule: mechanistic biomarker and pathophysiological studies can remain bounded in translational maturity if claims are explicitly limited and the evidence chain, declarations, article-type-specific transparency checklist, and reproducibility summary are complete.
